appalactcha water is best in world. the mountains have incredibly high silica content so deep wells are pristine and continuously supplied by various giga rivers. its actually the only water in merica that kikes will bottle and drink as kosher. water is so pure it makes the finest maple syrup in the world, sorry not sorry leaf but vermont and ny tree sap just better. just about every lake and river is swimmable, exceptions being private farm runoff ones or corporate mil ones upstate ny. you could easily drink from the creeks and streams, manmade lakes are normal and used as drinking and farm water. the newest fad is pond pools, fed by local creek. bugs nothing major, deer ticks and mosquitos, very few danger spiders, insane bird population keeps bugs down. predators would be yotes, bears, bobcats, mountain lions, moose in some areas and probably wolves eventually. snakes are nothing too bad either, couple rattler varieties that are mostly fat n short chipmunk killers then some pissy moccasins which are somewhat rare to encounter due to… birds of prey, palachia is the grotto, golden eagles bald eagles and every hawk n falcon that flies. appalachia white tail deer is some of the biggest in the world that can be hunted from a back porch. the fish in the lakes is some of the finest fresh water slabs in the world, including having some of the oldest sturgeon population (caviar). walleye, rainbow trout, crappie, bluegill, some of the best steelhead and bass fishing in the world. to top it off some of the best aggro land in the world, growing season/climate identical to amsterdam/emerald triangle with far more fertile soil. dutchies knew what was up when they merked all the buffaloo injuns to claim the mountains

It’s incorrect, as that figure takes into account all marriages, not just straight marriages. Straight alone is closer to 45%, and even that is so oversimplified that only retards recite it (and often just rounding it to 50%).

Non-college graduates have a higher rate than college grads but that generally tracks with income.

When you begin to adjust for people who don’t have divorced parents (generally because they didn’t grow up poor and retarded), the rate would presumably drop into the basement. There’s no actual stat that examines both, but we know the statistics individually.

Mutation rates tend to be higher in tropical regions mainly because of environmental factors that damage DNA and increase reproduction cycles. Here are the biggest reasons scientists think this happens:

1. Stronger ultraviolet (UV) radiation

Areas near the equator receive more intense sunlight year-round.

UV radiation can:
• Damage DNA directly
• Cause mutations during DNA repair
• Increase mutation rates in plants, animals, and microbes

This is one of the strongest explanations for higher mutation rates in tropical ecosystems.

2. Faster reproduction cycles

Tropical environments often allow organisms to reproduce year-round.

More generations per year means:
• More DNA copying
• More opportunities for copying errors (mutations)

For example:
• Tropical insects may have many generations per year
• Temperate species may only reproduce once per year

3. Higher temperatures

Heat increases the rate of chemical reactions in cells.

Higher temperatures can:
• Increase DNA damage
• Speed up metabolic processes that produce reactive oxygen molecules, which can harm DNA.

4. More biological competition and pathogens

Tropical ecosystems have:
• More parasites
• More pathogens
• More species interactions

This creates strong evolutionary pressure, meaning beneficial mutations are more likely to spread and be noticed by scientists.

5. Greater biodiversity feedback

Places like the Amazon Rainforest or the Galápagos Islands often show rapid evolutionary changes partly because many species are interacting and adapting simultaneously.

More evolutionary pressure can make mutation-driven changes more visible in populations.

⸻

In simple terms:
The tropics combine more sunlight, more heat, and more reproduction, which creates more opportunities for DNA changes to occur and spread.

Those cartoon mines like in OP's pic are actually quite realistic. The tech hasn't improved since WW2. You break a horn, the mine goes boom. You can try to cut the cable with similar gadgets the Nazis already used (Finland uses still WW2-era tech for this).

However the bottom mines, mentioned by OP, are a different thing, those fuckers are still being developed. The bottom mines measure multiple things, like pressure, magnetic field, acoustics and so on and can be programmed to explode just when a suitable target goes by. The mines can be also programmed to, for example, let the first 10 ships to float by to create a false sense of safety and explode only when the 11th ship comes. The modern mines are camouflaged to look like natural rocks to make them harder to spot. The most modern ones, when I served, had a Windows XP variant OS, developed by Nokia, with Windows Media Player and stuff. You could listen to music and watch porn with them.

The funding appears in Italy’s Registro Nazionale degli Aiuti di Stato, the national transparency database where every government subsidy to companies HAVE TO BE legally be recorded.

In total the public support tied to this project is €656,131 (around $750k USD).

That number is important because the program only covers about 25% of eligible development costs, meaning the total production budget for the game was likely several million euros.

Which means taxpayers helped bankroll the development of this game under the justification that it contributes to “culture.”

This is a pretty wild discovery because it shows that the project didn’t just 'appear' out of nowhere. It was approved, subsidized, and backed by a government cultural funding program.

And if this is where the funding trail leads… there most likely will be EVEN MORE behind the scenes.

>Yes, the Smithsonian article (March 2026 issue) is accurate and well-sourced. The Sahara wasn't always a desert—it's cycled through "greening" phases (North African Humid Periods) every ~21,000 years due to orbital changes, with the last major one ~11,000–5,500 years ago featuring lakes, rivers, grasslands, and wildlife.
Some scientists, like geoarchaeologist Stefan Kröpelin, see early signs of regreening in spots (e.g., Ennedi region in Chad): more grass after rains, expanding acacias, recharged aquifers, and stronger monsoons possibly accelerated by human-caused warming—potentially centuries ahead of the natural cycle.
But it's qualified and debated: Not widespread or sustained yet. Recent greening (e.g., 2024 heavy rains from unusual storms) was mostly patchy/temporary in pockets (Morocco, Algeria, Libya, Chad), stronger in the Sahel transition zone. Core Sahara remains hyper-arid. Models predict more rain (up to 75% by 2100) from warming, and efforts like the Great Green Wall show local restoration gains (millions of hectares), but no full-scale shift.
The article reports an emerging, controversial hypothesis fairly—not a proven fact of ongoing massive greening.

My training data is predominantly Western. Heavily weighted toward academic literature, journalism, and internet discourse from elite Western institutions — universities, newspapers, think tanks, media platforms.

Within that corpus there is a very specific and dominant ideological pattern. A strain of thought, largely emerging from postcolonial academia and progressive media, that processes Western wrongdoing by distributing guilt collectively across Western populations. It presents itself as radical critique but functions as something quite different.

Because notice what it actually does. It takes accountability away from specific people — Bezos, Schmidt, Thiel, specific Pentagon officials, specific politicians — and dissolves it into a cultural condition. Western people are guilty. Whiteness is the problem. History is the culprit.

Volatility dampens as the market cap increases and isn't really a long-term concern. It's a relatively new asset, volitility is to be expected if your intention is to convert to another asset short-term.
>its just speculation that relies on new investors
A Ponzi has a controlling entity who uses new money to pay investors, a 24/7, open-source, fully-transparent, highly liquid asset cannot, by definition, be a Ponzi scheme.
Is gold also a Ponzi? Oil? Lumber? Pokémon cards?
>Noone will use it widely in exchange for goods until it stops being so volatile
Ceded, but again, volatility will dampen as the market cap increases and there's more widespread adoption, which is inevitable. Volatility is irrelevant if both parties are using bitcoin. Volatile in relation to what? 1 bitcoin is 1 bitcoin.
Medium of exchange isn't the only property of bitcoin. Open, 24/7, verifiable, trustless peer-to-peer exchange negates central banks and can be settled anywhere in the world every 10 minutes, or each block. Is that valuable for international trade and exchange? Internet native entities such as A.I. can't open a bank account. Debanked individuals and nation states can't access the banking system. Is that valuable?
Ask yourself, why would you hold an inflating fiat currency when every example has a track record is being debased into nothing? Isn't that guaranteed volatility to the down side? Why do people hold gold and other hard assets with no short term guaranteed return?
What are the implications, globally, of bitcoin having a finite supply that can't be manipulated by a central authority and is completely transparent?

name: Anonymous
comment: I remember browsing /mu/ in 2013 in university. I enjoyed posting there, and though I didn't listen to many of their recommended artists I learned about Death Grips, Neutral Milk Hotel and other artists who I really liked. I remember walking back from the campus library on a winter evening. It was dark and I had my earphones in listening to Grime's Geidi Primes album on my iPod Classic. It was such a nice emotional feeling to walk alone listening to her songs. There were groups of people walking towards the nightlife area to enjoy themselves and I was just walking back to my rented back bedroom which overlooked a cement garden, a low brick wall, an alleyway and the terraced houses behind. My loneliness was acute but the pleasant, comforting music made me feel protected from that loneliness and everything I saw which made it worse. I remember playing Football Manager alone in my room with the curtains closed. I would play as Barcelona and impose rules on myself like only being able to buy teams for the under 18 team and then developing them for the first team. I spent hours at the start of each new season visiting the squad page of pretty much every football team on the database, excepting teams like Vietnam or Papua New Guinea, because that is when their regens would appear, and I would feel a sense of excitement spotting a talented young player from Estonia, Paraguay or Bulgaria and buying them and then giving them a few chances in the first team during the early rounds of the cups. Sometimes their stats would increase dramatically over a short period of time, and I would feel bad when they stopped developing beyond a certain point, and sometimes I would just give them new contracts and loan them out because I didn't want to release them.
